title: Symmetries and overparametrization properties of Hamiltonian variational ansatzes for the $(1+1)$d $\mathbb{Z}_2$ lattice gauge theory
authors: Kanta Yamanaka, Takanori Daiza, Katsumi Imaizumi, Yutaro Iiyama, Lento Nagano, Ryu Sawada, Koji Terashi
abstract: We perform detailed studies of five Hamiltonian variational ansatzes (HVA) based on the Hamiltonian of the $(1+1)$d $\mathbb{Z}_2$ lattice gauge theory. The ansatzes are designed to respect local and global symmetries of the original Hamiltonian and therefore act on a finely segmented state Hilbert space. Following Larocca et al. (2023), we numerically study the dimension of the dynamical Lie algebra (DLA) and the rank of the quantum Fisher information matrix (QFIM) of the ansatzes within specific invariant subspaces. The ansatzes all involve sums of weight-three Paulis in their generators, which is a feature that have so far been underexplored in this context. We also perform numerical experiments to determine the ground state energy of the original Hamiltonian via variational quantum eigensolver (VQE), and observe that overparametrization of the ansatzes coincides with the apparent disappearance of local minima in the loss function, in line with the finding in the reference. Finally, the decay rate of the VQE loss function under gradient descent optimization is revealed to scale linearly with the number of parameters in the ansatz. These results help to enrich the theory of overparameterization of quantum circuits and inform the design of scalable variational ansatzes.
summary: The paper rigorously analyzes Hamiltonian Variational Ansatzes applied to a lattice gauge theory, focusing on how symmetries constrain the variational space. It establishes that overparametrization leads to the saturation of the QFIM rank, which is bounded by the system's underlying algebraic structure (DLA dimension). This provides theoretical guidelines for designing more efficient and robust quantum algorithms.
main_problem: The study investigates the relationship between symmetries, overparametrization effects, and optimization dynamics when using Hamiltonian Variational Ansatzes (HVAs) to simulate quantum systems.
main_result: Overparametrization causes the QFIM rank to saturate at a value bounded by the dimension of the Dynamical Lie Algebra (DLA), while the VQE loss function decay rate scales linearly with the number of parameters.
method: The authors analyze five different HVA families applied to the $\mathbb{Z}_2$ LGT, calculating the DLA and QFIM rank, and simulating the optimization dynamics using Gradient Descent on the VQE loss function.
model_or_system: The physical system is the $(1+1)$d $\mathbb{Z}_2$ lattice gauge theory (LGT). The Hamiltonian is complex, involving Pauli operators, and the analysis focuses on restricting it to symmetry-invariant subspaces for variational simulation.
key_observables: Dimension of the Dynamical Lie Algebra (DLA), Rank of the Quantum Fisher Information Matrix (QFIM), VQE loss function decay rate ($\kappa$), and asymptotic loss values ($L_X$).
important_parameters: Number of parameters ($M$), number of layers ($L$), lattice size ($N_s$), and the degree of overparametrization.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on comparing theoretical bounds (DLA dimension) with numerical diagnostics (QFIM rank saturation) and assumes certain approximations regarding circuit design (e.g., 2-designs for decay rate scaling).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the dependence of DLA dimensions and saturated QFIM ranks on lattice size, show the convergence/saturation of QFIM rank versus ansatz layers, and plot the linear scaling of the loss function decay rate with the number of parameters.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes five HVA families by calculating their symmetry-constrained subspaces (DLA) and then uses these results to predict how the QFIM rank saturates as the circuit depth increases, correlating this saturation with the observed behavior of the VQE loss function during optimization.
why_interesting: This work provides crucial theoretical diagnostics for designing scalable quantum circuits by linking fundamental algebraic properties (Lie algebras) to practical computational metrics (QFIM rank and optimization convergence).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 4. Average decay rate κ of the loss functions for the five families of HVA with various number of layers L. The decay rate scales linearly with L. The slope of scaling is dependent on the number of lattice sites Ns.

FIG. 5. Second frame potential F2(Vj) of the ensemble of V+j(θ) matrices where θ is drawn uniformly from RLKX (X = A, B, C, D, E). Values are computed with Monte Carlo integration employing 10000 samples. Error bars are statis- tical. For larger parameter indices (shallower unitaries), out- of-range F2 values are represented by vertical arrows. Note that the vertical axis range is wider for ansatzes families D and E compared to the other three.
